🎤 FINAL INTERVIEW ANSWER

Choose anonymous types when you know the property names and types at compile time and the data never leaves the current method scope — ideal for LINQ GroupBy/Select projections inside a repository method. Choose dynamic when the structure is determined at runtime: deserializing JSON from payment webhooks with varying schemas, COM automation, ExpandoObject for plugin systems, or legacy APIs returning untyped data. Dynamic trades compile-time safety for flexibility — typos in property names become RuntimeBinderException in production. Modern alternative to dynamic for JSON: use JsonDocument, JsonNode, or System.Text.Json with JsonElement for structured access without full DTO mapping. In fintech I use anonymous types for internal report shaping, records for API contracts, and JsonDocument or source-generated JSON for external gateway payloads — dynamic is a last resort when schema varies unpredictably.

🧠 ONE LINE MEMORY TRICK

Shape known and local? Anonymous. Shape unknown until runtime? dynamic — or better, JsonDocument for JSON without the DLR tax.

questions.tip: Anonymous types = read-only, compiler-generated, scope-limited. Great for LINQ projections. For anything leaving a method, use a named DTO or record instead.

questions.question: What causes memory leaks in C#? How do you detect and fix them?
questions.answer:
📌 SIMPLE DEFINITION

Memory leaks in .NET occur when objects stay alive due to unintended root references — the GC cannot collect them because it sees them as still in use. Common causes: unsubscribed event handlers, static collections that grow forever, undisposed IDisposable objects, and closures capturing large objects. Like leaving doors open that the GC cannot close because it thinks someone is still using the room.

✅ ADVANTAGES

AdvantageWhy It Matters
Knowing leak patterns prevents design flawsIDisposable + unsubscribe built into event-subscribing classes from day one
dotnet-counters for live GC monitoringWatch Gen2 size trend in production without restarts
dotnet-dump for heap analysisFree production heap snapshots — dumpheap -stat finds growing types
WeakReference for safe cachingCache exchange rates without preventing GC collection under memory pressure
using statement prevents resource leaksSqlConnection always disposed — no connection pool exhaustion
IMemoryCache with expirationReplaces unbounded static lists with auto-evicting caches

🏦 FINTECH EXAMPLE (C#)

Payment dashboard subscribes to TransactionMonitor events but never unsubscribes — thousands of disposed Dashboard instances accumulate, causing OOM at peak trading times.

public class DashboardFixed : IDisposable
{
    private readonly TransactionMonitor _monitor;

    public DashboardFixed(TransactionMonitor monitor)
    {
        _monitor = monitor;
        _monitor.TransactionReceived += OnTransactionReceived;
    }

    public void Dispose()
        => _monitor.TransactionReceived -= OnTransactionReceived;

    private void OnTransactionReceived(object? s, TransactionEventArgs e) { }
}

void GoodMethod()
{
    using var conn = new SqlConnection(connStr);
    conn.Open();
}

Console.WriteLine("Dashboard disposed — event unsubscribed, connection released");
// Output: GC can collect Dashboard; connection returned to pool immediately

📊 DIFFERENCE TABLE

Leak CauseDetectionFix
Event handlersHeap snapshot growthUnsubscribe in Dispose()
Static collectionsGen2 size growthIMemoryCache with expiration
Undisposed IDisposableConnection pool errorsusing statement
Closure capturesLarge objects in Gen2Capture only what you need
Async state machinesGrowing Task objectsAwait Tasks, use CancellationToken

🎤 FINAL INTERVIEW ANSWER

Memory leaks in .NET happen when the GC cannot collect objects because live references still point to them. The four most common causes: event handlers not unsubscribed — the publisher holds the subscriber alive via the delegate; static collections that grow without bound — no eviction policy; IDisposable objects not disposed — database connection pool exhaustion; and closures capturing large objects that stay alive while the delegate is alive. Async methods can also leak: long-running Tasks holding captured variables in their state machine, async void event handlers, and infinite loops without CancellationToken. Detection: dotnet-counters to watch Gen2 size trend, dotnet-dump to take heap snapshots and run dumpheap -stat, dotMemory or PerfView for deeper analysis comparing baseline vs post-reproduction snapshots. Prevention: IDisposable + unsubscribe in Dispose() for event subscriptions, IMemoryCache with sliding expiration for shared caches, using statement for all IDisposable objects, WeakReference for caches that can be regenerated, always await Tasks and prefer async Task over async void.

🧠 ONE LINE MEMORY TRICK

Four open doors the GC cannot close: events (close with -=), static caches (close with expiration), connections (close with using), closures (close by not capturing large objects).

followUps.question: How do you use dotMemory or PerfView to find a memory leak?
followUps.answer:
📌 SIMPLE DEFINITION

dotMemory and PerfView are memory profiling tools that capture heap snapshots and let you compare object counts between baseline and post-leak states to find types growing without bound. The workflow: take a baseline snapshot, reproduce the leak, take a second snapshot, diff to find surviving objects and their GC roots. Think of it as comparing two bank vault inventories — if 5000 Dashboard objects should have been cleared but still appear, you found the leak.

✅ ADVANTAGES

AdvantageWhy It Matters
Snapshot comparison finds growing typesDiff Gen2 heap before and after load test — type with 10x instance count is the suspect
GC root paths show why objects survivedotMemory shows Dashboard is held alive by TransactionMonitor event delegate chain
PerfView is free from MicrosoftCollect ETW heap snapshots in production-like environments without JetBrains license
Integration with dotnet-dump for productionTake dump on live server, analyze in dotMemory offline — no profiler attach needed

🏦 FINTECH EXAMPLE (C#)

Payment dashboard memory leak: dotMemory snapshot before load test shows 50 Dashboard instances; after 1000 page loads without snapshot diff shows 1050 — event handler root path reveals TransactionMonitor subscription.

// Reproduce leak scenario:
for (int i = 0; i < 1000; i++)
{
    var dashboard = new LeakyDashboard(_monitor);
    dashboard.Render();
    // dashboard goes out of scope but monitor holds reference
}

// dotMemory workflow:
// 1. Run app under dotMemory
// 2. Force GC (Collect Allocated Memory)
// 3. Snapshot #1 (baseline)
// 4. Execute reproduction loop above
// 5. Force GC again
// 6. Snapshot #2
// 7. Compare -> LeakyDashboard count grew
// 8. Inspect GC roots -> event handler chain

public class LeakyDashboard
{
    public LeakyDashboard(TransactionMonitor m)
        => m.TransactionReceived += (_, _) => { };
}

🎤 FINAL INTERVIEW ANSWER

To find a memory leak with dotMemory: attach to your running ASP.NET payment API or launch under dotMemory, take an initial heap snapshot after forcing GC, reproduce the leak scenario (load test 1000 dashboard requests), force GC again, take a second snapshot, and use Compare Snapshots to see which types increased in instance count and total size. Click the growing type and inspect Incoming References or GC Root paths to see what holds objects alive — usually event handlers, static dictionaries, or cached closures. PerfView workflow is similar: collect a GC heap snapshot ETW trace with /GCOnly, open the snapshot in PerfView, use Heap Snapshot diff to find growing types, and follow root paths. For production, use dotnet-dump collect on the live process, copy the dump, and open in dotMemory or Visual Studio Diagnostic Tools. Key signal: Gen2 collection size grows linearly with request count and never drops after forced GC.

🧠 ONE LINE MEMORY TRICK

Two vault inventories minus each other — whatever pile grew between snapshots is your leak, follow the GC root chain to who is hoarding it.

followUps.question: What is a WeakReference and when does it help with caching?
followUps.answer:
📌 SIMPLE DEFINITION

A WeakReference holds a reference to an object without preventing the garbage collector from collecting it — the GC can reclaim the target when memory is needed, and WeakReference.Target returns null afterward. Use it for caches where regeneration is acceptable — exchange rate lookups, computed report data, or large object caches that should not force OOM under pressure. Think of it as a bank's optional memo of a customer's phone number — if the customer moves (GC collects), the memo quietly goes blank instead of keeping the apartment occupied.

✅ ADVANTAGES

AdvantageWhy It Matters
Cache without preventing GCExchange rate cache entries collected under memory pressure instead of causing OOM during peak trading
Automatic eviction by GCNo manual TTL needed for low-priority cached data — GC acts as natural eviction policy
IsAlive and Target check validityBefore using cached object, check Target != null — regenerate if collected
WeakReference is typed and saferGeneric version avoids casting and clearly communicates cached object type

🏦 FINTECH EXAMPLE (C#)

FX rate cache uses WeakReference so cached decimal rates survive for fast lookup but get collected during memory pressure instead of blocking GC from reclaiming megabytes.

public class FxRateCache
{
    private readonly ConcurrentDictionary> _cache = new();

    public decimal GetRate(string currency, Func fetch)
    {
        var weak = _cache.GetOrAdd(currency, _ => new WeakReference(0));

        if (weak.TryGetTarget(out var rate) && rate > 0)
            return rate;

        rate = fetch(currency);
        _cache[currency] = new WeakReference(rate);
        return rate;
    }
}

var cache = new FxRateCache();
var usd = cache.GetRate("USD", c => 83.5m);
Console.WriteLine($"USD rate: {usd}");

🎤 FINAL INTERVIEW ANSWER

WeakReference is a wrapper that references an object without making it strongly reachable — the GC can collect the target object during collection even while the WeakReference itself survives. Check IsAlive or TryGetTarget before use; if null, regenerate the cached value. Use WeakReference for caches where stale data is acceptable and you want memory pressure to naturally evict entries — computed analytics, deserialized config objects, or image thumbnails. Do NOT use WeakReference for objects that must stay alive for correctness — active payment sessions, open database transactions, or security tokens. Modern alternatives: IMemoryCache with size limits and sliding expiration for ASP.NET apps; ConditionalWeakTable for attaching metadata without extending object lifetime. WeakReference solves the specific problem of "I want to cache this if memory allows, but not cause a leak if memory is tight."

🧠 ONE LINE MEMORY TRICK

WeakReference = optional memo the GC can erase — great for nice-to-have caches, never for must-stay-alive payment state.

followUps.question: Can async methods cause memory leaks? How?
followUps.answer:
📌 SIMPLE DEFINITION

Yes — async methods can cause memory leaks when long-running Tasks hold references to captured variables, event handlers use async void, or Tasks are fire-and-forgotten without awaiting. The compiler-generated async state machine stores captured locals as fields that live until the Task completes — if the Task never completes, neither does the captured object graph. Think of it as a bank hold on a customer's account that never gets released because an async authorization Task is still pending indefinitely.

✅ ADVANTAGES

AdvantageWhy It Matters
Captured variables in state machineAsync lambda capturing HttpClient and large byte[] lives until Task completes — hours if gateway hangs
async void event handlersException and state machine survive for app lifetime — subscriber never garbage collected
Fire-and-forget Task without trackingBackground payment retry Task holds DbContext until completion — thousands accumulate under load
CancellationToken prevents infinite holdsPass token to cancel hung API calls — state machine completes and releases captures

🏦 FINTECH EXAMPLE (C#)

Payment webhook handler starts fire-and-forget async Task capturing DbContext — under burst traffic thousands of pending Tasks hold contexts alive, exhausting the connection pool.

public class WebhookProcessor
{
    private readonly AppDbContext _db;

    // LEAK: fire-and-forget captures _db in state machine
    public void OnWebhookReceived(WebhookPayload payload)
    {
        _ = ProcessAsync(payload); // not awaited — Task holds _db reference
    }

    // FIX: await, or use IHostedService with scoped context
    public async Task ProcessAsync(WebhookPayload payload)
    {
        await _db.Payments.AddAsync(new Payment(payload.Id, payload.Amount));
        await _db.SaveChangesAsync();
    }

    // async void LEAK in event handler:
    // _monitor.OnEvent += async (s, e) => { await LongRunning(); };
}

record WebhookPayload(string Id, decimal Amount);
record Payment(string Id, decimal Amount);

🎤 FINAL INTERVIEW ANSWER

Async methods cause memory leaks through several mechanisms. First, the compiler-generated state machine class captures all local variables and this references used across await points as fields — these live until the Task completes. A fire-and-forget Task from an event handler that captures a large DbContext or HttpClient keeps it alive indefinitely if the Task hangs waiting for a payment gateway. Second, async void methods used as event handlers cannot be awaited and their state machines survive for the application lifetime — always use async Task for event handlers. Third, registering async lambdas on events creates closures that capture the subscriber, preventing GC. Fourth, infinite loops in background Tasks without CancellationToken never complete, permanently holding captured state. Fixes: always await Tasks or track them in a registry; use IAsyncDisposable for async cleanup; pass CancellationToken to all async calls; avoid async void except UI event handlers; use scoped DI for DbContext in background work via IServiceScopeFactory instead of capturing a long-lived context.

🧠 ONE LINE MEMORY TRICK

Async leak = a Task holding your stuff hostage until it finishes — fire-and-forget plus captured DbContext equals connection pool graveyard.

questions.tip: The #1 memory leak in C# is unsubscribed event handlers. Always implement IDisposable if your class subscribes to events, and unsubscribe in Dispose(). Use using statements for everything IDisposable.
